message_content: Hello! We are interested in your personal opinion and your own experiences with AI in your daily work. Feel free to ask if anything is unclear. The interview will take about 30 minutes. To start, could you tell me what kind of work you currently do and how long you've been in your role?
i'm a paralegal at a mid-sized law firm, mostly working on contract review and litigation support. been there almost nine years now
Thank you for sharing that. Nine years gives you good perspective on how things have changed. Could you describe whether AI tools have entered your daily work, and how?

yeah pretty significantly in the last two years. the firm rolled out a contract analysis tool around early 2024, and then they added an AI research assistant maybe eight months ago. now we use them for first-pass document review, summarizing depositions, drafting routine clauses. some of the senior associates also use chatgpt and claude for brainstorming arguments, though that's more unofficial
That's a substantial shift. When you say first-pass document review, what would that have looked like before these tools arrived? And what does it look like now?

before, a case with say 50,000 documents in discovery, we'd have a team of four or five paralegals plus contract reviewers spending weeks tagging documents for relevance and privilege. now the AI does an initial pass overnight, flags what looks privileged or relevant, and two of us spot-check its work. so the same case takes maybe a week. honestly the work itself is less mind-numbing but theres also less of it to go around
That last point is interesting. Have you noticed staffing changes at the firm since these tools came in, or in your hiring patterns?

they havent done layoffs but they havent replaced people who left either. we used to have eleven paralegals in litigation support, now we're seven. two retired, two went to other firms, and the positions just quietly didnt get refilled. the firm calls it natural attrition but everyone knows what's happening
How does that pattern affect your sense of security in your own role? Do you feel your position is stable, or uncertain?

honestly its complicated. i feel pretty safe for the next year or two because i handle the complex stuff the AI cant do well, like coordinating with clients, managing case strategy with the attorneys, the judgment calls. but five years out i genuinely dont know. the tools keep getting better fast. last year it couldnt do deposition summaries worth anything, now they're decent. so what about next year
You mentioned the AI cant do certain things well. Could you give specific examples of where the tools fall short and you have to step in?

sure, the contract analyzer is good at finding standard clauses but it misses context. like last month it flagged an indemnity clause as standard, but the underlying transaction was unusual and that clause actually created huge exposure for our client. i caught it because i remembered the deal structure. also it hallucinates sometimes, especially with case citations. one of the associates almost filed a brief with a fake citation a couple months ago
That fake citation incident sounds serious. How did your firm respond, and did it change anyone's behavior with AI?

they put out a written policy that any AI output had to be verified by a human before going into a filing. honestly people were already doing that mostly, but the partners got really nervous after seeing news stories about lawyers getting sanctioned. theres now a verification log we have to keep. its more paperwork but probably a good idea given what almost happened
Do you feel the verification process is actually catching errors, or has it become a checkbox exercise people rush through under deadline?

bit of both honestly. when its a high stakes filing people genuinely review carefully. for routine stuff i think people skim and sign off. ive caught a few errors that someone else's verification missed. its hard because the AI is right most of the time so you start trusting it, and thats exactly when it bites you with something subtle
Has your training or what you needed to learn for the job changed? How did you actually learn to work with these tools?

the firm did a half day training when they rolled out the contract tool, which was mostly marketing from the vendor. the real learning came from just using it and figuring out what to trust. i also did an online course on prompt engineering on my own time, paid for it myself. younger paralegals coming in seem to pick it up faster, they grew up with these tools, and i think thats starting to matter
When you say it's starting to matter that younger people pick it up faster, do you mean for hiring, for who gets the better assignments, something else?

for assignments mainly. the partner running the latest big case asked specifically for paralegals who were comfortable using the AI tools. i was on that list but a couple of my colleagues werent. so they got put on more traditional work, which honestly is shrinking. i worry about them. one is in his late fifties and i dont think he wants to retrain on yet another piece of software
Has the pace of work itself changed? More cases per person, faster turnaround, or has the pace stayed similar?

definitely faster. clients now expect contract reviews back in two days that used to take a week. partners take on more matters because the throughput is higher. so even though the AI does some work, im not really working less, im just working on more things in parallel. its more cognitively demanding actually because im constantly switching contexts and reviewing AI output is a different kind of attention
That last point about reviewing AI output being a different kind of attention is interesting. Could you say more about that?

when i used to read documents myself i was building understanding from scratch, my brain was engaged the whole time. now im reading something the AI wrote and im looking for what could be wrong. its like proofreading versus writing. you can miss things that arent there, omissions, things that should have been flagged. you also start unconsciously trusting the framing the AI gives you, and then you're stuck inside its perspective without realizing it
Do you and your colleagues talk about these issues? Is there a forum at work to discuss how AI is changing the job?

informally over coffee or lunch, all the time. officially almost never. theres a tech committee but its mostly attorneys deciding what tools to buy. paralegals dont really get consulted about how the work changes, even though were the ones using these things eight hours a day. id like it if our input was actually sought out instead of just announced to
Has your compensation changed in line with the increased throughput, or has the productivity gain mostly gone to the firm and clients?

ha. raises have been about the same as before, maybe three percent a year. the firm is doing better financially, billable hours are up across the firm even with fewer people. partner profits per equity partner went up significantly last year, that was in the firm announcement. so no, i wouldnt say the gains have flowed down. thats a sore point for a lot of us
Is there any union, professional association, or collective voice for paralegals at your firm or in your region?

no union. theres a state paralegal association but its mostly continuing education and networking, not really representation. legal industry isnt very unionized in general. i sometimes wonder if that should change, but its hard to organize people who all aspire to maybe go to law school or get promoted into management
Looking at clients, do they know AI is being used on their matters? Has that conversation come up in any way you've seen?

some clients ask, especially the bigger corporate ones with their own counsel. they want to know what tools were using and what data is going where. theres been some friction about confidential documents going through third party AI services. the firm has an enterprise contract that supposedly keeps data segregated but i dont think clients fully trust that and honestly i dont blame them
Have you ever felt uncomfortable about something an AI tool was being used for in your work? What happened?

yeah a couple times. one was when an associate wanted me to use the AI to draft a strongly worded letter to an opposing party, and the tone it produced was way more aggressive than i thought was appropriate or accurate to the facts. i pushed back and we toned it down, but i could see how someone less experienced would just send what the AI generated. another time i wasnt sure if a summary it produced was actually accurate or just plausible sounding
That second concern, plausible sounding versus accurate, seems pervasive. How do you actually verify when youre unsure?

i go back to the source documents. if its a deposition summary i pull up the transcript and check the cited pages. if its a legal point i look up the actual case. its more work but its the only way i trust it. the problem is when youre under deadline, the temptation is to skim. ive started timing my reviews so i dont skip the verification step
How do you feel about the broader public conversation around AI and jobs? Does it match what you see day to day?

the public conversation feels split between hype and doom and neither matches my experience. its not replacing me tomorrow but its also not just a tool like spell check. its something in between, its restructuring the job in ways that are hard to see from outside. fewer entry level positions, more demands on remaining workers, productivity gains going to ownership. thats the real story but its less exciting than robots taking over
You mentioned fewer entry level positions. What concerns you about that, both personally and for the profession?

the entry level work, the document review, the basic research, thats how people learned the trade. you built up your judgment by doing thousands of hours of grunt work. if the AI does that work, where do new paralegals build that intuition? i worry were going to have a generation that can prompt AI tools but cant tell when the output is wrong because they never developed the underlying judgment. and ten years from now there wont be enough senior people to catch the mistakes
Thats a thoughtful concern. If you could change one thing about how your firm has rolled out AI, what would it be?

id involve the people doing the work in the decisions. paralegals know what the AI gets right and wrong better than the partners who approved it. i would also be more honest about the staffing implications instead of pretending its all about efficiency. and id share the gains. if the firm is making more money per worker, some of that should go back to the workers, not just to the equity partners
What about regulation or government policy? Do you think anything should be done at that level, and if so what?

im not a policy person but a few things seem obvious. transparency about when AI is being used, especially for clients. some kind of disclosure when AI generates legal documents. maybe support for workers who are displaced. and i do think we need rules about data, what client information goes into these tools and where it ends up. right now it feels like every firm is making it up as they go and clients are bearing the risk
Looking back at the last two years of these changes, has your overall feeling about AI at work shifted, or has it stayed roughly the same?

ive gotten more nuanced i think. early on i was either excited or scared depending on the day. now im more matter of fact about it. its a tool that does some things well and some things poorly. it changes the work in ways thats partly good and partly bad. the bigger issue isnt the technology its how the gains and losses get distributed and who has a say in that. those are political questions, not technical ones
Do you have advice for someone entering your field today, knowing what you know about how AI is changing it?

learn the AI tools deeply, dont just use them. understand where they fail. but also build the human skills the AI cant do, client relationships, negotiation, judgment about ambiguous situations. and dont assume the path will look like mine did. fewer paralegals will mean more competition for the remaining roles, but also potentially more responsibility for those who stay. be ready to keep learning constantly
Is there anything we havent covered that you think is important about how AI is showing up in your daily work?

one thing. theres a loneliness to it i didnt expect. used to be that document review was tedious but you did it in a room with other people, you talked, you built relationships. now im alone with the AI most of the day, reviewing its output. the office feels emptier. that social fabric of work is fraying and nobody talks about it because its hard to put on a productivity dashboard
Thats a striking observation and not one ive heard often. Thank you for taking the time to share all of this, your perspective has been really thoughtful and helpful.
thanks for listening. it was actually nice to think this through out loud
